description:
You are a compliance officer who has just logged into the machine to perform an historical-configuration audit.  
    A directory tree already exists at  

        /home/user/audit_targets/  

    that contains a mixture of old and new “*.conf” files spread across several nested folders.  
    Your goal is to locate ONLY those “*.conf” files whose modification time is **strictly more than 30 days in the past**, create one-for-one backup copies of each of them using the same name plus a “.old” suffix, and then produce a formal log of everything you did.  
    Because hundreds of files may match, you MUST solve the task with the GNU utilities “find” and “xargs” (or “find  …  -exec … +”) so the work happens in batch rather than by an explicit loop over individual paths.

    EXACT REQUIREMENTS
    1.  Discovery  
        • Search must begin at /home/user/audit_targets (recursively).  
        • Select only files whose names end in “.conf”.  
        • Restrict the result further to those whose mtime is **older than 30 full days** (i.e. “find … -mtime +30”).  

    2.  Backup  
        • For every file that matches, create a copy that lives in the *same* directory and has “.old” appended to the original file name.  
          Example:  /some/path/foo.conf  →  /some/path/foo.conf.old  
        • If a “.old” file with that exact name already exists, silently overwrite it.  
        • You may **NOT** move or rename the originals – only copy them.  
        • All copies must preserve the original file’s contents byte-for-byte (permissions may differ).  

    3.  Compliance Log  
        • Make sure directory /home/user/compliance_logs/ exists (create it if necessary).  
        • Write a plain-text log file called  

              /home/user/compliance_logs/backup_operation.log  

          that follows the exact format shown below.  
        • The file is *case-sensitive* and lines must appear in precisely this order:

            Line-1:  Backup Operation Compliance Log
            Line-2:  Original|Backup|Status
            Line-3+: One line per file you actually backed up, lexicographically sorted
                     Each data line = <absolute-original-path>|<absolute-backup-path>|OK
            Final line:  TOTAL_BACKED_UP=<integer-count>

          Example layout (note: paths below are illustrative only):

            Backup Operation Compliance Log
            Original|Backup|Status
            /home/user/audit_targets/db/mysql.conf|/home/user/audit_targets/db/mysql.conf.old|OK
            /home/user/audit_targets/webserver/httpd.conf|/home/user/audit_targets/webserver/httpd.conf.old|OK
            TOTAL_BACKED_UP=2

        • “Status” must literally be the text “OK” for every file line.  

    4.  Verification hints  
        • Use GNU find’s “-print0” plus “xargs -0” (or the “-exec … +” form) so paths containing spaces are handled correctly.  
        • Make sure the final log is readable with `cat /home/user/compliance_logs/backup_operation.log`.  
        • The automated grader will inspect:  
            – that only the files older than 30 days have “.old” counterparts,  
            – that the “.old” files have identical content to their originals, and  
            – that the log exists, follows the stated format exactly and lists the correct files in sorted order with the right TOTAL_BACKED_UP count.

    You do NOT need root privileges for any part of the task.
truth:
<!--
        PRE-EXISTING DIRECTORY STRUCTURE & FILE CONTENTS (timestamps already set)

        /home/user/audit_targets/
        ├── webserver/
        │   ├── httpd.conf          (mtime 40 days ago)
        │   ├── newhttpd.conf       (mtime  2 days ago)
        ├── db/
        │   └── mysql.conf          (mtime 35 days ago)
        └── app/
            ├── settings/
            │   ├── app.conf        (mtime 34 days ago)
            │   └── recent.conf     (mtime  1 day ago)
            └── archive/
                └── oldapp.conf     (mtime 90 days ago)

        EXACT CONTENTS of the four “old” files (each ends with a single LF):
        ---------------------------------------------------------
        /home/user/audit_targets/webserver/httpd.conf
        ServerName localhost
        Listen 80

        /home/user/audit_targets/db/mysql.conf
        [mysqld]
        port=3306

        /home/user/audit_targets/app/settings/app.conf
        debug=false
        api_key=XYZ123

        /home/user/audit_targets/app/archive/oldapp.conf
        version=1.2.3
        ---------------------------------------------------------

        Files newer than 30 days must NOT be backed up:
            /home/user/audit_targets/webserver/newhttpd.conf
            /home/user/audit_targets/app/settings/recent.conf

        EXPECTED RESULT AFTER SUCCESSFUL EXECUTION
        • The following four backup files must exist and be byte-identical to their originals:
            /home/user/audit_targets/webserver/httpd.conf.old
            /home/user/audit_targets/db/mysql.conf.old
            /home/user/audit_targets/app/settings/app.conf.old
            /home/user/audit_targets/app/archive/oldapp.conf.old

        • The following log file must exist and contain exactly seven lines,
          the middle four sorted lexicographically by original path:

        /home/user/compliance_logs/backup_operation.log
        ---------------------------------------------------------
        Backup Operation Compliance Log
        Original|Backup|Status
        /home/user/audit_targets/app/archive/oldapp.conf|/home/user/audit_targets/app/archive/oldapp.conf.old|OK
        /home/user/audit_targets/app/settings/app.conf|/home/user/audit_targets/app/settings/app.conf.old|OK
        /home/user/audit_targets/db/mysql.conf|/home/user/audit_targets/db/mysql.conf.old|OK
        /home/user/audit_targets/webserver/httpd.conf|/home/user/audit_targets/webserver/httpd.conf.old|OK
        TOTAL_BACKED_UP=4
        ---------------------------------------------------------

        • None of the “recent” files should have a “.old” counterpart.

        • The test harness will run diff(1) to verify content identity and exact log layout.
    -->
name: task_000000_5cc0f6f2
